fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to get this web-native bridge toolkit wrapped up. The core idea is that our mobile app embeds web content and right now every team that uses it ends up writing the same boilerplate glue code to talk between the web layer and the native side — parsing messages coming in, building reply messages going out, figuring out why a page failed to load, that kind of thing. It's a mess and everyone's doing it slightly differently.

We need a clean library that handles all of this consistently. Things like reading structured data out of web messages, sending replies back, dealing with those annoying JSON converter situations (you know the two cases we always hit), summarizing page load responses without dumping the whole HTML, categorizing HTTP failures and browser-level errors and SSL cert problems, and then the path config + routing logic — basically the same approach we used for the route matching in the login flow, just applied here.

One thing that keeps biting us: when HTML is really long we don't want to log the whole thing, just enough context from both ends. And the routing needs to handle those sneaky credential-stuffed URLs properly — we got burned by that before.

The whole thing needs to be structured properly, not one giant file. Tests should drive it. Can someone pick this up and make sure all the edge cases from the existing test suite are covered?

Quick follow-up from the questions that came in: for the HTML summary bit, the preview should be based on normalized HTML, meaning whitespace is collapsed and newlines are stripped before we decide whether to shorten it. From the test behavior, a 26-character string is not truncated, but a 75-character string is. When it does get shortened, we keep roughly the first 26 characters and the last 26 characters of that normalized HTML and join them with ' [...] '. Separately, response_length should still report the original byte/character length before normalization, accurately.

Also on bridge payload encoding failures, there are only two buckets we should emit and they need to stay stable. If there is no converter at all, that is 'no_json_converter'. If a converter is present but not actually a valid/functional converter object, that is 'invalid_json_converter'. In those cases we should not leak runtime exception names or generated messages — the output should just be 'error=no_json_converter' or 'error=invalid_json_converter'. Same thing reflected in feature3_bridge_payload_encoding_errors.json: converter='none' producing error=no_json_converter, and converter='invalid' producing error=invalid_json_converter.

For the routing question, if the URL has userinfo before the host, like 'https://app.my.com@fake.domain', we need to compare against the real authority host, which is 'fake.domain', not 'app.my.com'. So start_host matching should always use the correctly parsed target host. And just to make this explicit: decision and matches are separate. route_kind drives decision every time — 'app' means decision=navigate no matter what, and 'external_browser' plus 'external_browser_tab' mean decision=cancel no matter what. Then the 'matches' field is just whether the parsed target host equals the parsed host of the start location, true or false. That’s the same behavior called out in feature9_navigation_route_classification.json: comparing the parsed host of the target URL against the parsed host of the start_location URL, with decision determined by route_kind (app=navigate, external_browser/external_browser_tab=cancel) and matches determined by host equality.

Last thing on payload decoding: the only built-in schema we support right now is 'title_subtitle'. That expects a JSON object with 'title' and 'subtitle' string fields and should output exactly 'title=<value>\nsubtitle=<value>\n'. If the schema name is unknown or unsupported, like 'unsupported_empty_shape', we should return 'data=null\n' instead of throwing. The idea is the core decoder stays narrow and future schemas can be added via extension without changing that core path.

trigger_keywords: what fields are preserved when replacing a bridge message
which parts of the original message carry over to the reply
does the original event or json_data survive a message replacement
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When replacing a bridge message, the fields preserved from the original are: id, component, and metadata_url. The event is always replaced by the supplied replacement_event. The json_data is replaced either by a raw replacement_json string (used as-is) or by serializing the replacement_data map to a JSON string. The original event and original json_data are discarded.

trigger_keywords: what HTTP status codes have known reason phrases
which status codes have blank reasons
is 406 Not Acceptable or something else
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Known reason phrases must match exactly as specified in the test contract. Notably, 406 maps to 'Not Accessible' (not the standard 'Not Acceptable'). Status codes 430 and 499 are in the client error range (400-499) but have no known reason phrase and must output an empty reason string ('reason=\n'). Any status code outside 400-499 and 500-599 is classified as category=unknown.

trigger_keywords: how is path configuration loaded for URL lookup
where does the path config data come from
what does the path properties lookup return for each URL
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Path configuration is loaded from a bundled/embedded JSON configuration that contains rules and settings. The lookup returns a merged set of properties for each URL matched against the rule patterns: context, presentation, query_string_presentation, title, pull_to_refresh_enabled, and uri (native fragment destination). The output also includes rules_count (total number of rules loaded), settings_count, and any custom settings (e.g. setting_custom_app_feature_enabled=true). Properties from multiple matching rules are merged, with later/more-specific rules overriding earlier ones.

trigger_keywords: what SSL certificate error codes are recognized
what is the category for an unknown SSL error code
how many SSL error codes map to named categories
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: SSL certificate error codes 0-5 map to named categories: 0=not_yet_valid, 1=expired, 2=id_mismatch, 3=untrusted, 4=date_invalid, 5=invalid. Any code outside this range (e.g. -1 or 6) maps to category=other with an empty description string ('description=\n'). This differs from web load errors which use category=unknown for unrecognized codes.

trigger_keywords: what is the codebase structure expected to look like
should this be a single file or multiple modules
how should the project directories be organized
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project must be organized as a multi-file, multi-module structure (not a single god file) reflecting the distinct responsibilities: bridge message/payload domain models, payload decoding, message replacement, response summarization, HTTP/web/SSL error classification, path configuration, navigation routing, and a separate I/O adapter layer that handles JSON command parsing and output formatting. The core domain logic must be fully decoupled from stdin/stdout concerns. A recommended layout includes src/ for domain modules, tests/ for test cases, and a thin adapter/runner entry point.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'decode_bridge_payload', 'replace_bridge_message', 'encode_bridge_payload_with_converter', 'summarize_visit_response', 'classify_http_status', 'classify_web_load_error', 'classify_ssl_certificate_error', 'lookup_path_properties', 'classify_navigation_route'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: The middle-truncation algorithm for HTML previews in feature4_visit_response_summary.json: preserve a prefix and suffix of the normalized HTML and join them with ' [...] ', while separately reporting the original pre-normalization character length as response_length

URLs containing userinfo/credentials in the authority component (e.g. 'https://app.my.com@fake.domain') where the real host is 'fake.domain', tested in feature9_navigation_route_classification.json case 3

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't fully specified in the current ticket — you'll need to look at the existing test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and cross-reference the PRD in start.md to determine the exact expected behavior. The test cases are the authoritative contract for output format and edge case handling.

feature1_bridge_payload_decoding.json.description: Decode bridge message JSON payloads into a requested structured payload shape, returning the decoded fields or a null data marker when the payload cannot satisfy that shape.

feature2_bridge_message_replacement.json.description: Create an outbound bridge message by preserving the incoming identifier, component, and metadata while replacing the event and either a raw JSON payload or an encoded structured payload.

feature3_bridge_payload_encoding_errors..description: Report language-neutral error categories when a structured bridge payload cannot be encoded because JSON conversion support is missing or invalid.

feature4_visit_response_summary.json.description: Render a visit response summary with status, normalized HTML preview, middle truncation for long responses, and original response length.

input.response_html: <html><head></head>This is a really long response that is truncated.</html>

cases.expected_output:
status=200
response_html=<html><head></head>This i [...] that is truncated.</html>
response_length=75

input.response_html:
<html>
<head></head>This   is a really long response that is truncated.
</html>

cases.expected_output:
status=200
response_html=<html><head></head>This i [...] that is truncated.</html>
response_length=79

feature5_http_status_classification.json.description: Classify HTTP response status codes into client, server, or unknown error categories while preserving the numeric status and known reason phrase.

cases.expected_output:
status=505
category=server
reason=Http Version Not Supported

feature6_web_load_error_classification.j.description: Classify browser page-load error codes into stable categories and descriptions, using an other category for unrecognized codes.

feature7_ssl_certificate_error_classific.description: Classify SSL certificate validation error codes into stable certificate-error categories and descriptions, using an other category for unrecognized codes.

feature8_path_configuration_lookup.json.description: Load navigation path configuration and return the merged properties that match each requested URL path, including settings, context, presentation, title, refresh behavior, and destination URI.

feature9_navigation_route_classification.description: Classify a requested URL against the application start host for in-app navigation or external handoff routes, preserving the route decision and match result.
